failure here and so the want he has at the start of the story is you know I just want to be normal I want to fit in with other people I don't want to let my single mom down because she's already got a hard enough life as it is as the story progresses though and if if you're not familiar with the plot of Percy Jackson and the Lightning Thief it's basically about this kid discovering that he is in fact the son of a God and that the Greek gods are alive and kicking and they still influence the

world he needs to accept his true identity as a demigod and find the courage to act for himself not as a porn of circumstance not as a porn of the Gods around him so the truth he needs to come to accept by the end of the story is that by embracing my courage I can be a true hero and I can lead others to achieve greatness we're actually be returning to Percy Jackson a little bit later on in this video as I break down the plot structure and how that interacts with his character Arc as well so keep an eye out for that later in

this video now when it comes to structuring a negative character Arc the character is going to begin the story by fully believing the truth and they already have their need so you would think great this person's sort it they don't need to worry about anything but it's not quite that easy what happens is the LIE is introduced into their life and they become tempted by this thing there's a seductive nature to the lie here the character begins to chase their want they ignore the need they already

have and the LIE begins to overtake this truth the climax offers them one last pivotal chance to reject the lie and return to the truth but unfortunately the want is too tempting the LIE is too strong they fully embrace the lie and they reject the truth an example of this is co Shivers in best served cold by Joe abomi which is this kind of like Revenge fantasy version of Kill Bill is the best way to describe it co Shivers is one of two major point of view characters in this story and he goes through this Arc

of naivity to courage to love to despair to anger it's quite a complex arc when you actually break it down his ghost here is that he comes into the story as someone who had this very violent past as a warrior in the North and the truth he has is that he can build a new peaceful life if he stops using violence to find purpose at the start of the story he basically has immigrated to this new country of steria and he hopes to put his violent past behind him by you know just becoming like a guy down

here and not being a warrior anymore his need is to let go of his anger and his ego and move on from his violent past and again at the start of the story you know he's on track for this he's arriving in steria he's excited for this new beginning but as he goes through steria you know he starts getting disrespected by people there he finds he doesn't have the same kind of fearsome reputation he did when he was a warrior in the north and he gets sick of this he gets sick of being you know abused and

robbed and derided and that sort of thing and so he begins to want to regain his respect and to regain his power and also to develop a sense of affection from monzo Mado who is the the main character of this story who basically is this really influential mercenary who gets a bit too influential and is almost assassinated by Juke Oro so she sets off in this quest for Revenge to kill ooso and the other people that were involved in the conspiracy against her and shivers basically gets recruited to help

her into that for him he sees it as a way to regain his purpose the LIE he gets at the end end of the story is really perfectly encapsulated in this dialogue he has with Monza in the climax where he says reckon I've learned just to stick in the place I'm at just to be the man I am what's that Monza asks a killer he says so then the last type of Arc you can write is a flat Arc so with a flat Arc your character Begins the story by fully believing the truth and they already probably have their need

well sorted however the world believes the lie so they believe the opposite to what the character does as a result there's conflict and the world is going to to reinforce the LIE which results in your character's truth being challenged of course because this is a flat Arc and your character's progression and change is not really the me your focus here they remain steadfast they are still sticking to the truth this leads to two options for the climax option one is that the truth triumphs your main

character manages to basically persuade the world to adopt their same truth the second option is that the LIE is too strong for the truth the truth is vanquished and your character is defeated perfect example of this is Ned Stark from A Game of Thrones he has this Arc of honor conviction to death you know spoilers for the first book here but I think you probably know what happens anyway his ghost is his loyalty to King Robert Baratheon you know they're good friends King Robert barathan is obviously the ruler of

Westeros and they work together to overthrow the previous King but that ghost is going to come back to haunt Ned here the truth Ned has is that we must do what is right no matter what the cost and his need at the start of the story is to remain true to his Oaths however the lie that the world presents to him is that sometimes our Oaths should actually bended for the greater good and you have many other characters who literally say this to him in the story and the want that is kind of presented to him to tempt him is to stop Westeros

from falling back into war now of course cuz this is a flat Arc Ned stays unwaveringly committed to his truth and his need he remains true to his Oaths even though he probably should have bent those Oaths and you know done a bit of stuff that would have gone against them in order to prevent westos from falling back into war but because he is unable to change because his Arc is flat he is executed at the end of the story he basically gets outplayed by other people who are willing to bend their Oaths for

what they perceive to be the greater good okay so the next thing to talk about and to understand is story structure in my mind structure or a plot and your character's Arc or the journey that your character goes on need to be considered in unison the best stories are the ones where you can't really separate between character and plot instead there is a sense of cohesion between them so that is the whole aim of npoint story structure which is a approach I've developed over the years I've been writing as a way to sort of

seamlessly merge together the external Journey a character goes on with the internal transformation they experience over the course of the story as well so we're going to go through each of these nine points here and I'm also going to show you how these nine points are used to structure Percy Jackson and the Lightning Thief which is a very well structured story so that you can understand the actual principles and deeper purposes behind each of these structural points so just as a quick refresher on what this story is about

Percy Jackson and the Lightning Thief follows a young 12-year-old boy who discovers he is the demigod son of Poseidon and that the Greek gods are alive and kicking in our modern world this first book in the series focuses on Percy and his friends Annabeth and Grover as they Quest Across America in an attempt to retrieve Zeus's stolen lightning bolt from Hades who they believe has taken this bolt in order to instigate a war between the gods one thing that's important to note right from the start is that you'll see here

I've got these rough percentage markers at the top you don't always need to be hitting the exact structural moment at the exact percentage mark that is just unrealistic in many cases however you will typically find that a lot of the points I'm going to discuss here will fall around these benchmarks but it's more about just moving through all of these nine points in order and using it as a rough guideline a rough scaffold that helps you build a structure when you're creating a house no one is

building the scaffold for the sake of building the scaffold they're using the scaffold as a way to actually construct that second story as a way to fix the roof or to get the tiles up there that is the same approach you want to have so the first point is the hook so the hook is where you introduce your main character and especially their flaws desires key traits you know personality perspective all of that sort of stuff that makes them feel unique in a compelling way you ideally want to show

a somewhat characteristic moment here a moment that immediately explains to the reader what makes this person different what makes them special what makes them interesting to follow in the rest of the story so in in the case of Percy Jackson our opening Hook is we meet Percy who is a troubled 12-year-old kid he has 80hd and dyslexia and he feels like a huge Outsider he only really has one other friend this guy called Grover who also seems a bit like an outsider a bit of a misfit as well and on a school trip to a

museum his teacher turns into a harpy and attacks him Percy manages to defeat this sort of evil school teacher but then he is left wondering if it even happened at all because you basically have all the other people at his school asking him like why are you talking about M dods we don't have a m dods we've got this other teacher name instead the only person who seems a little sus here is his friend Grover who seems to hesitate when he repeats the same lie so this brings us to the inciting incident this is where the

Ordinary World of your character's lives is disrupted by a key event that sets the story in motion so in the case of Percy Jackson while camping on a trip with his mother Percy's friend Grover appears and Grover basically says he's being chased by this monster called The Minotaur so this you know horned big bull combined with a man from Greek mythology and Percy's mother basically realizes that she needs to drive him to this place called Camp Half Blood where apparently he will be safe there and at

this point Percy is starting to realize that things aren't all normal in his life and there may be some secrets about his upbringing or about his birth that his mother has not told him however before they are able to arrive at this Camp the Minotaur Takes her and she vanishes in a Flash of light Percy basically thinks his mother has been killed at this stage Percy somehow manages to kill the monster and he passes out right afterwards is the last few paragraphs that conclude this scene the last thing I remember is collapsing

on a wooden porch looking up at a ceiling fan circling above me moths flying around a yellow light and the stern faces of a familiar looking bearded man and a pretty girl her blonde hair curled like a princess's they both looked down at me and the girl said he's the one he must be and you can see here this comes on page 59 of my addition about 16% of the way through the story this then leads into the entering of the new realm this is where your protagonist is taken from their Ordinary World and

thrust into the extraordinary world of your story now Ordinary World extraordinary world you can just take those terms as metaphorical in some stories there will be a very literal very clear transition between the Ordinary World and the extraordinary world something like Harry Potter is a good example Harry goes from the Muggle world into the Wizarding World but in other stories like maybe a detective story where there is a crime that comes along that is sort of more brutal or more violent or more devious than

anything the detective has dealt with before that can also signal a transformation from The Ordinary World where the main character kind of understands the rules into the extraordinary world where the character is off balance they're out of their comfort zone and there're so much more danger and threat as a result of that so in the case of Percy Jackson this transition occurs when Percy wakes up in Camp Half Blood and he discovers that he is a demigod he is the son of a human mother and one of the Greek gods is his

father father or mother he doesn't quite know at this stage who his Godly parent actually is but he basically learns that all the Greek myths are real and the Greek gods are alive in modern day America he's told that the big three Gods so Zeus Poseidon and Hades they are not allowed to have kids because there's this dangerous prophecy that if one of their kids gets to the age of 16 they will destroy Olympus and he is introduced to the other demigods in the camp including Annabeth who is a girl

who sort of helps retrieve him after his initial confrontation with the minor she is a child of Athena and initially she's a little bit kind of edgy towards him another key figure he meets is Luke who is a child of Hermes and Luke is a bit of a older Mentor figure who takes Percy under his wing and helps to sort of introduce him into Camp life under Luke's guidance Percy is tested to try and find out which God he belongs to but unfortunately he sucks at almost everything here so he still continues to

feel like a huge Misfit next we move on to the fourth story structure point which is the antagonist threatening your main character so this is where pressure is applied to your main character typically through either the sort of introduction or just some kind of key action from your antagonist so you might have actually introduced your antagonist earlier on maybe you even introduced it in the hook of your story right at the start but this is your opportunity to tighten the screws on your main character and to elevate the level of

threat they face for example in Percy Jackson during a game of Capture the Flag which is a training exercise that all the demigods in Camp Half plot are going through Percy is ambushed by Clarice and some other children of Aries Clarice is kind of a bit of a bully figure towards him she's a very strong Warrior and they don't really get along too well the start of the book now Percy thinks he's going to die or be horribly maimed at this point because the Aries children have a real grudge against him

but by entering a creek of water he somehow gains enough strength and skill to miraculously defeat them and this actually lets his team win the game of Capture the Flag he finds out that he was used as bait by anabeth so that Luke that Mentor guy I was mentioning from before could win so Percy feels a little bit bent out of shape for being used like bait in this way and unfortunately he doesn't have too long to Relish in his victory because immediately after this he is attacked by a huge hellhound

this kind of massive doglike creature sent from the underworld however he is saved by the campers and then receives a very critical sign let me read this scene to you now Percy Annabeth said pointing by the time I looked up the sign was already fading but I could still make out the Hologram of green light spinning and gleaming a three- tip spear a trident your father Annabeth murmured this is really not good it is determined Chiron announced Kiron by the way is the centur who is the leader and supervisor of this Camp

All Around Me campers started kneeling even the Aries cin although they didn't look happy about it my father I asked completely bewildered Poseidon said Chiron Earth Shaker stormbringer father of horses hail pereus Jackson son of the sea God now this is really not good news because it is established in the story that the big three Gods so Zeus Poseidon and Hades are forbidden from having kids because of this prophecy that if one of their kids makes it to the age of 16 they will destroy Olympus and you'll see

here this particular Scene It takes place about 33% of the way through now this leads to the next kind of reveal which elevates the threat of the story further where Chiron basically says that Hades sent a minion to steal the master Bolt the master bolt is the principal weapon of Zeus it's kind of the lightning bolt that all of the lightning BTS are formed from and it's a major component of Zeus's power however it's been missing and if we continue on with this scene here he hit it in the

Underworld knowing full well that Zeus would blame Poseidon I don't pretend to understand the lord of the Dead's motives perfectly or why he chose this time to start a war but one thing is certain Percy must go to the underworld find the master bolt and reveal the truth so this basically sets up the main quest of the story we learned that someone has stolen Zeus's lightning bolt they've framed Poseidon for it quite a few of the Gods think that Percy is the one who has stolen this device and what

you'll see here is that it really elevates the danger that Percy faces because he can't just stay sheltered in Camp Half Blood in the security and safety that provides he now needs to Quest Across America to get to the entrance to the underworld which is hidden in Los Angeles in this story and go down to basically retrieve the bolt from Zeus so the Threat Level has massively increased here and from a structural perspective that's exactly what you want to be thinking about at this antagonist threatens moment you

want to be asking yourself how do I Elevate the stakes and the difficulty that my main character is facing in order to force them to grow and to progress along their Arc so this basically leads to Percy Annabeth and Grover setting off on their Quest leaving Camp Half Blood and journeying west across America our next structural moment is the midpoint Revelation so here in a moment of insight your main character is going to finally understand a really important truth about themselves and if you remember when we

were talking about character arcs before we discussed the difference between your character's lie and your character's truth the midpoint is the moment where they finally understand the truth maybe for the first time but they're not able to fully relinquish the LIE yet they can't quite let go of the lie because it's got its roots deep into them now this is a very crucial moment for your entire story you can almost think about this midpoint as the pivot that your entire story revolves around and from a

character Arc perspective it's a really crucial moment a really crucial opportunity to show your character going through the process of change if you've struggled to develop character arcs where it feels like a character is changing in an organic and natural way and it feels like your character is just changing on a dime it's probably because you haven't worked out how to write an effective midpoint Revelation and a book that I found really useful for clarifying my own thinking about this

was actually James Scott Bell's write your novel from the middle to quote from James here in the midpoint of your story the character is forced to look at himself as if in a mirror only it's a reflection of who he is at that moment in time who am I what have I become come what do I have to do to regain my Humanity sometimes it's the character looking at the odds how can I possibly win it looks like I'm going to die physically or spiritually now what am I supposed to do that right there is the

kind of emotional goal of a good midpoint in your story so how does this actually apply in Percy Jackson well the midpoint I would say here comes after they defeat Medusa so this sort of creature who if you look at her in the eyes it would turn you to Stone Percy Annabeth and Grover managed to defeat her and they sort of this quiet reflective moment afterwards to bond and just think about the quest that they're actually going on here we learn a bit about Grover's motivations for coming along to help protect Percy here as

shown in this scene the god of wild plac has disappeared 2,000 years ago Grover told me but for the satas and Grover by the way is a SATA so they kind of look like a human but they have the clothing hoofs of a goat but for the satas pan was our Lord and Master he protected Us in the wild places of the earth we refused to believe that he died in every generation the bravest sers pledge their lives to finding pan they searched the Earth exploring all the wildest places hoping to find where he is hidden and

wake him from his sleep and you want to be a Searcher Percy asks him it's my life's dream he said so this is an interesting thing to keep in mind with your midpoint Revelation typically the midpoint is going to be a more quieter contemplative moment where your characters you know might be sitting around a campfire after a Long Day's travel or after a big fight and just reflect ing on what's happened how they're changing the difficulties that lie ahead of them and it doesn't just

have to be your main character that goes through this moment of introspective reflection in fact as you can see here the midpoint is an excellent opportunity to develop Your Side characters in a little bit more detail as well and to get them to maybe start opening up to the reader so some of the other things to think about with your midpoint here from a structural level are it's a really great opportunity to do a bit of reflection on the odds we see this quote here from Percy Jackson how are we going

to get into the underworld I asked Grover I mean what chance do we have against a God in Percy Jackson They also use this as an opportunity to develop more sympathy towards Annabeth who at the start of the story is a bit of a jerk towards Percy but we have here this line from Grover where he says don't be so hard on her Percy she's had a tough life but she's a good person and then we also get a bit of added complication here as well so after the crew are attacked by the furies which are these

sort of Servants of Hades down in the Underworld we get this exchange between Grover and Percy maybe but Annabeth and I we both got the feeling they weren't asking about a person they said where is it they seem to be asking about an object that doesn't make any sense Percy said I know but if we've misunderstood something about this Quest and we only have 9 days to find the master Bol Grover looked at me like he was hoping for answers but I didn't have any so you can see here it's a very simple dialogue

exchange but it does a great job of just reminding us of the stakes reminding us of the timeline that they've got 9 days to find the master bolt and also adding this extra hint of worry that maybe they don't fully comprehend what they're trying to achieve here and then lastly and I know I'm spending a long time on the midpoint but it is a very very critical element of your story and it's something I often see writers skip over or not give the attention it deserves lastly we see Percy Express his true

motivations for the quest so remember Percy Begins the story thinking that he just has a single mother and he's never known his dad he obviously finds out later on that his dad is bidon the Greek god of the sea and we get this particular Exchange between him and Grover I thought about what Medusa had said I was being used by the gods what lay ahead of me was worse than petrification so obviously that's just internal thought that Percy's having here I haven't been straight with you I

told Grover I don't care about the master Bol I agreed to go to the underworld so I could bring back my mother who was of course sort of turned into that light and vaporized by the Minotaur earlier on in the story Grover blew a soft note on his pipes I know that Percy but are you sure that's the only reason I'm not doing it to help my father he doesn't care care about me I don't care about him Grover gazed down from his tree Ranch look Percy I'm not as smart as Annabeth I'm not as Brave as

you but I'm pretty good at reading emotions you're glad your dad is alive you feel good that he claimed you and part of you wants to make him proud that's why you mailed Medusa's head to Olympus you wanted him to notice what you've done and see here how this comes almost smack bang right in the middle of the story it comes here at the 49% Mark bit it's pretty much in the middle of this book and it really is this quiet moment that pivots the main character's Arc and shows you sort of what Percy is

seeking for here there is a element of him sort of seeking for his father's approval so when it comes to writing the midpoint in your own story I want you to be thinking about what is an aspect of my character's motivations that I can perhaps reveal or explore here or make evident to the character that they didn't realize they were pursuing earlier on in the story and this leads us to our six structural moment which is the Amplified pressure moment so this is where your protagonist you know in the

midpoint they started to slowly understand a bit more about the truth and to combat the lie that has been you know weighing them down throughout their life so far however in the Amplified pressure moment your protagonist's new understanding of the truth is going to be challenged by your antagonist basically ramping up the intensity and the danger of the lie so your character might be forced to backslide a little bit away from the truth and towards the lie in the case of Percy Jackson this happens when they encounter Aries the

God of War who basically lead them into this trap now Percy manages to get the others out of this particular trap and Aries gives him this information as a reward he says that Percy's mother is in the Underworld as a hostage and he then gives them this backpack full of supplies here is the exchange between Aries and Percy in this Sim so Percy asks my mother's been kept why you need to study War Punk says Aries hostages you take somebody to control somebody else nobody's controlling me he laughed

oh yeah see you around kid so you remember earlier on that a lot of the arc that Percy goes through in the first book is going from a position of feeling like his life is out of his control and the gods are manipulating him circumstances has manipulated him and just generally feeling quite helpless and then by the end of the story he kind of reaches this point of being able to step up to take responsibility to take charge and to use his courage as a way to actually Forge his own path forward so you see here Aries is kind of like

taunting Percy and dragging him back towards the lie that he is helpless that he is powerless and that he is being manipulated by all the people out there now pretty much straight after this we go into a dream sequence where Percy hears the voice of this monstrous creature in Hell which is where they're heading they're heading into the underworld he hears this monstrous creature talking about how Percy is walking right into the Trap that they' have set here's that scene and he suspects nothing it asked another voice

one I almost recognized answered at my shoulder nothing my Lord he is as ignorant as the rest I looked over but no one was there the speaker was invisible deception upon deception the thing in the pit mused aloud excellent Poseidon has played his most desperate card now we shall use it against him shortly you shall have the reward you wish and your revenge as soon as both items are delivered into my hands and you can see here this Falls at the 64% Mark of the story so again it's ramping up the threat and the pressure of the

antagonist it's making Percy doubt himself it's making him question whether walking right into the underworld as they are planning to do is a smart move and it is ramping up the difficulty that the character faces as we move towards the end of the story so this brings us to point number seven the darkest low this is where your character kind of gets into a bit of a pit it's the most suffering that theyve faced so far another way of thinking about this is the kind of Dark Night of the Soul you

may have heard that in other structural examples and at this point in the story the LIE has never looked so strong we set course at the stage for the final confrontation between your protagonist and the antagonist so in the case of Percy Jackson This Moment occurs when Percy Annabeth and Grover all finally head into the Underworld for the first time I'll read that scene to you now the shoreline of the underworld came into view craggy rocks and black volcanic sand stretched Inland about 100 yards to

the base of a high stone wall which marched off in either direction as far as we could see a sound came from somewhere nearby in the green Gloom echoing off the stones the howl of a large animal and you can see here this happen right at the 73% mark of the story so again you know these percentage markers don't always have to be like bang on accurate to the 9o structure I showed you before but you will often find when you analyze great stories out there they do tend to fall really close to those

markers and whether or not that's because the author is consciously aware of this structure or whether it's just because subconsciously this structure kind of Springs from the patterns of story we're so used to telling over hundreds and thousands of years of human history either way it just shows you how moving through these nine points is going to result in a story that just feels right and in the case of Percy Jackson I mean how much more structurally perfect can you get for your darker low moment to be when your

characters are literally heading into the underworld I think it's just really really nice how that works out here so this leads us to our climax now the climax is pretty straightforward It's the final confrontation between your protagonist and your antagonist in this final confrontation either the truth is going to win or the LIE is going to win what's important here is that one of those Concepts is vanquished and the other one Reigns Supreme so in the case of Percy Jackson while they're in the

Underworld they confront Hades to get the bolt that they think he stole from Zeus to frame Poseidon but it's a trap and the bolt is actually inside Percy's backpack he's actually brought it down to Hades here's the scene where he discovers this I was speechless I had no Helm I had no idea how the master bolt had gotten into my backpack I wouldn't to think Hades was pulling some kind of trick Hades was the bad guy but suddenly the World Turns sideways I real I'd been played with Zeus Poseidon and Hades had

been set at each other's throats by someone else the master bolt had been in the backpack and I'd gotten the backpack from and this is where Percy realizes that Aries set him up Aries gave him the backpack earlier on in the story and so Percy realizes that he has been manipulated in just the way the Aries warned him about so Hades at this point you know he has control of Percy's mother who was sent to the underworld by the Minotaur before and Hades offers this deal he says to Percy that he can

return his mother if Percy returns hades's Helm which has also been stolen but Percy doesn't really know anything about this he's just finding this out at this stage in the story so Percy makes a very tough choice at this point to basically leave his mother in the Underworld for the sake of the quest and using three pearls that they were given by God earlier Percy Grover and anabeth all return to the surface and here they confront Aries on the beach here's the scene where they do that there he was

waiting for us in his black leather duster and his sunglasses an aluminium base bat propped on his shoulder his motorcycle rumbled beside him its headlight turning the sand red Hey kid Aries said seeming genuinely pleased to see me you were supposed to die you tricked me I said you stole the helm in the master ball now what follows from this is a really cool fight scene between Percy and Aries in the surf Percy manages to sort of use the powers of Poseidon to defeat Aries or at least get a blow on him which then makes Aries

get angry and leave essentially as per the terms of their fight but what Percy realizes is that there's something off about Aries and he realizes that Aries has also been manipulated by someone outside of him too so it's not like Aries is the big villain here he's also just being a porn in somebody else's game and this in turn leads to the big reveal and the big twist of the story which is that it's actually Luke the mentor the child of Hermes who was a friend to Percy earlier on in Camp Half

Blood and showed him the ropes and kind of gave him guidance he's actually the one who set this all up he tells Percy that he hates the Gods he leaves camp and almost gets Percy killed with this poisonous scorpion right before he leaves and Percy just manages to survive thanks to getting into the water in time and then getting rescued by some other Camp members but now Percy knows that there is a bigger threat out there and so he knows that Luke is working with Kronos who is the ruler of the titans

the the father of all the gods who you know was was cut up into a million pieces basically by Zeus and is wanting to come back and get his revenge and this essentially sets up the entire Conflict for the whole first five book series this brings us to our last structural moment which is the closing image so this is where you see your protagonists living with their new truth and need they've gone on this experience over the course of the story from moving away from their lie and their want in

order to accept the truth and their need and this often times but not always will have some sort of mirroring to your opening image as a way to show the change and the transformation that your character has gone on so in the case of Percy Jackson Percy went from being this Misfit kid who was an outcast at the start to now having a camp full of friends who are just like him and he understands his kind of unique traits and actually appreciates them the threat of Kronos and Luke is on the horizon out

there he knows that not all is safe and that there are bigger threats they're going to have to deal with however Percy has also managed to inspire some change in the side characters around him at the start of the story Annabeth is estranged from her dad and at the end of the story Percy sees Annabeth going off to sort of reunite with her dad on the border of Camp Half Blood and then you know leave for the end of the season and we get this nice little closing seen in the last few pages when I get back next

summer she said so this is Annabeth we'll hunt down Luke we'll ask for request but if we don't get approval we'll sneak off and do it anyway agreed sounds like a plan worthy of Athena she held out her hand I shook it take care seaweed brain Annabeth told me keep your eyes open you too wise girl so what we basically see here and this is something I probably didn't cover heaps in this structure but is a major component of the book as well sort of more as like a subplot you basically see Annabeth going

from sort of resenting Percy or being rude to him at the start to them actually forming quite a close friendship and then as the story continues there is a bit of a romance between them as well so the last thing I just want to talk about with structure before we move on to the next phase which is writing your first draft is that you should be using it as a guide you don't want structure to be something that is impeding you or Heming you in instead I actually find that structure is just like scaffolding when you're

building a building it is something that helps you design shapes and put things together in a way that you couldn't if you were just assembling it all without the of this scaffold around it so use structure as inspiration don't feel too stressed about hitting each of these nine points I've mentioned at the specific percentage markers of your book just focus on taking the story through those particular points and when you do it you will often find that that actually makes the story flow in a much

more natural and much more cohesive way particularly if you're someone who struggles to figure out what to do in the middle of your story or how to kind of get between a to B then look at those structural moments in more detail particularly those points between number three and number seven and that will actually give you a lot of guidance and a lot of inspiration for how to fill in those scenes and to not make the story feel like it just jumps from the start to the end too quickly okay so now that

you have developed your story sufficiently and hopefully built for yourself a good outline that's going to give you a lot of guidance it is time to move on to the actual writing of your first draft the first thing we need to talk about here is how to actually form a consistent writing habit that's going to help you make rapid progress with your first draft and get it done in a way where you feel very motivated to right it feels fun and the words just flow from you effortlessly because ideally you want to try to be getting

your first draft done in a couple of months rather than spending years to do it it's so much more motivating when you know that you're going to be able to finish this first draft a few months down the line rather than toiling away at it for a decade and it gives you more opportunity to then have more creative energy to come back and do the editing which is the really important work as far as I'm concerned now one thing I want to make sure that you're aware of right away is that it is possible to

improve your writing habits in quite a dramatic fashion I got this message from Stacy who went through my easy writing habits course a little while ago and she was saying how you know before she went through this course she was averaging about 100 words a day and after going through this course and you know implementing the things that I teach in there she is now writing anywhere between 1,500 to 2,000 words on her good days on her okay days it's around 1,000 to 1,500 words and on her bad days it's

around 800 to 1,200 words this is awesome and it really goes to show that you should not consider your capacity for how much you can write in a date to be fixed and in this next section of the video I'm going to be sharing with you the principles that you can be doing to get this same kind of transformation with your own habits so the first thing to be thinking about here is to have the right mindset heading into your first draft for your first draft your job is simply to shovel sand into the sandbox

it's not to craft these amazing sand castles or sculptures out of sand or anything like that just get the sand in there and then once everything is piled in you'll know what to work with that's when you can start refining the structures that you're creating in there the second key component of your mindset at this stage of the writing process should be to only focus on today's step it is so easy as a writer and I've fallen into trap this trap many times before to focus on you know what's it

going to be like when this story is published or what's the cover going to be or what is book four in this series going to be about or how many sales can this book make or something like that and every single time I've fallen into the Trap of thinking ahead of thinking too far forward of where I actually am with the writing process it has just hurt me what you have to be doing and this applies to you know every writer out there no matter whether you're just starting out or you're a bit deeper into

your writing career just focus on today's step and in many cases that action that you need to take today is simply just showing up and writing for an hour 2 hours whatever it is that you can make doe with in the day just sit down get the words down on the page don't Focus or worry about anything else because the thing is anything can be fixed with your story except a blank page so you want to end up with not a blank page now another key aspect of the first draft mindset particular particularly if this is the first book

that you're writing is to really just be focusing on this question of how can you get 1% better with your writing each day so sometimes you'll show up to your writing and you'll go today I want to write better dialogue I want to make it feel more distinctive for every character maybe another day you show up and you go you know what I want my setting descriptions to be less generic and less cliched and more immersive and more Vivid and more interesting maybe you show up another day and you go I'm

going to try to write in a way where I'm not using redundant words and I'm being as concise as possible I find that setting these little challenges for myself these opportunities for improvement every day it keeps me engaged in the story and if you just focus on getting 1% better with your writing every day by the end of a year you are going to be blown away with how much progress you've made the reason why I talk about all these mindset principles here is that writing particularly when you're starting out is

mostly about just getting the junk words out of your system it's a little bit like turning on a tap and a bunch of Gunk and and crap is just flowing out of it and so many riters at this stage of the process they'll see the gunk flowing out and they'll turn the tap off and they think that the tap is broken but if you just let the tap flow for a little bit longer eventually that Gunk clears away and what you get is beautiful clear you know great tasting water when you are starting out as a riter you just

have to get the junk out of your system and to do that you just need to be focusing on volume on consistency on showing up every day and putting those words down and not worrying really about where it's going to go because riding a book is like climbing a mountain if you're just looking at the peak all the time it doesn't feel like you're making any progress but if you just focus on the next step that you have to take that is the way to eventually end up at the top so your number one priority right

now as you're going through the process of writing your first draft is simply to just be enjoying the process again this is the single biggest hack that I know of in order to develop effortless writing motivation it's to be exploring a story that you're just so damn passionate about that you can't help from sitting down and writing at every opportunity you get or you're thinking about it when you're driving around or going for a walk or in the shower in the gym or wherever else it might be in your

life that is again I strongly recommend that you just focus particularly if this is your first book you just focus on writing a story that you're passionate about don't worry about whether it's marketable whether other people will like it whether it will sell just focus on exploring something that's incredibly fascinating to you and just Stokes your curiosity and your desire to know more to put it in the words of The Stormlight Archive it's all about focusing on the journey before the destination as Rachel

Aaron says in the end what matters most is that you're happy with your own writing because contrary to the myth of the tortured artist a happy writer will always produce more and better than an unhappy one one day you're going to look back at this process and this stage of the writing career that you're in right now with fondness pretty much every author I've talked to has always looked back at their first book with this great degree of affection and joy because you're discovering so many things and

the joy of riding is in the figuring it out I'm still figuring it out to this day but the amount of figuring out that you have to do with your first book is just astronomical and there's actually a lot of joy that comes from that a lot of growth a lot of progress a lot of flow so again don't Focus here on publication don't Focus here on Fame just focus on building the identity of a writer and learning to love the process and love the craft and love the process of getting better at the craft okay so with

that kind of fundamental mindset covered let's actually discuss how you can build a great writing habit the first thing you want to be thinking about is how can I find my energy Apex some people great first thing in the morning but if they try to get any work done after lunch they will just be sluggish and falling asleep other writers are going to be much more night hours and this is something where you just need to experiment with writing at lots of different times of the day lots of different places and contexts as well

because it's going to take a little while to find the sweet spot for what produces the best result for you I know when I was starting out as a writer earlier on like when I was writing across the broken stars for instance you know I would write from like 10:00 p.m. through to 2:00 a.m. in the morning and I would just get on these incredible kind of late night session where I would just be immersed in the story and i' be very uninhibited and I would just go from there I could probably do that at

the time cuz I was a 19-year-old student you know studying overseas in London but these days I'm much more of an early morning writer and I find that if I don't do my writing first thing in the morning it's much harder to then come by it later in the day you also want to be treating yourself here as a mental or creative athlete athletes do everything they can to optimize their life outside of the actual playing of their sport in order to make sure that their games can be as good as possible and one one of

the biggest levers that you can be pulling here is sleep to quote from Dr Matthew Walker sleep is the greatest legal performance-enhancing drug that few athletes are abusing enough if you're struggling to make progress with your writing it could just be that you're not getting enough high quality sleep right now the next thing to be thinking about here is to try to aim for daily writing as much as possible when you work on your story every day even if it is as little as like five sentences

or 50 words or whatever it is it just keeps the story lodged in your subconscious and it means that as you go about your tasks in between your writing sessions you are going to be percolating this story over in your mind and your subconscious is going to be bubbling up with all sorts of great ideas and insights and cool character moments and scenes and all that good stuff I find that when I go a few days between writing it's much less likely for me to get hit with this kind of creative Epiphany and on the occasion where you

might have to skip a day because sometimes life happens and you can't write every single day just try to have this mentality of no two skip days you know you missed a day's writing today okay I'm going to get back at it tomorrow and I'm going to try to write first thing in the morning even if I just get a couple of words down while my coffee is brewing or whatever it is it's just going to make sure that I'm not skipping 2 days in a row because the thing is once you skip 2 days in a row

it's very easy to skip three four five and then before you know it a whole week's gone by you've lost track of where you are in the story and you're not really making a lot of progress let's dig even deeper into the science of habit formation so an atomic Habits by James Clear he talks about this sort of four-stage loop that creates and maintains habits in our life now in terms of creating a positive habit these are the four stages right here so you can see we've got Q craving

response reward for the queue you want to make it obvious so that it's easy to start the habit for the craving you want to make the Habit attractive for you to do for the response you want to make it easy to do the Habit it's a lot easier to go for a 5 minute run than it is to go for a 2hour run for example and then the reward involves trying to make it as satisfying as possible that creates this Loop that hopefully reinforces itself over time and makes it easier and EAS iier to perform this habit so in the

case of writing for the que you would want to be thinking about like how do I make it obvious for me to do my writing every day maybe that means you know printing off a map of where your story takes place having that up somewhere in your house so you're always sort of thinking about your story maybe you draw some artwork about your characters or you just have some you know materials around that inspire you to begin the writing process in terms of the craving to make it attractive for yourself you

want to be asking what can I be doing to make today's writing session as fun as possible you want to be focusing on what it about the scenes you're writing today that's going to be fun to explore maybe it's the first time that these two characters meet each other or maybe you're going to have some great witty dialogue in there or perhaps this is going to be a demonstration of your cool magic system that you've not been able to write before focus on how you can make your writing as attractive as it

possibly can be every single day for the response you want to make it Easy by following what I call the 5 minute rule so if you tell yourself hey I have to sit down today and write 10,000 words or I'm a failure that's pretty hard and in most cases the difficulty of that is probably going to just stop you from beginning in the first place but if you follow the 5- minute rule of just saying look I'm going to sit down for 5 minutes I'm going to open up my word document I'm going to just read through what I've

done so far maybe type out a few sentences and then if I want to I can stop right there in most cases if you do that you actually kind of trick yourself into now being motivated to write because the thing is a lot of people think that you need motivation to perform an action what I have actually found from having written you know 1.2 million words over the last 12 years is that action creates motivation and 50% of the difficulty with writing is simply just sitting down and starting once you get that first sentence down you have

overcome 50% of the challenge that you're going to face for that day's writing session and it becomes so much easier to just keep going forward from that point and then in terms of the reward which is all about trying to make it satisfying so that you create this positive feedback loop for yourself a lot of writers just have the reward of you know finishing the first draft or maybe you know getting it published later on for example I like to think about how can I bring the reward forward

into my present as much as possible and chunk it up so that I'm not just having this one big reward at the end but I'm actually having lots of little wins throughout the process so to do this I like to track my word counts every day in a spreadsheet I've been doing this since 2018 and I basically just write down how many words I wrote each day how long I spent on my writing and if I spent any time you know editing or outlining as well I also include that in there too and it just sort of gamifies

the process it makes it so much easier to be motivated because you're able to see the these little winds stack up day in and day out and you create this sort of chain of consistent days where you've ridden every single day in a row and if you've never tracked your word counts before I think you'll be shocked at how much of a difference it makes to your motivation and to how fast you will progress with your writing for example here is a check-in post from Jack Harris who is one of the members of the fantasy

Writers Guild my accountability and support Community for Fantasy writers and you can see here in his first week inside the guild he wrote a total of 8,800 words in his first week which is pretty good and one of the things we actually do inside the gu is every Sunday we do a check-in post where we basically share what our progress has been like with our writing this past week so you can see here the second week that he was in The Guild Jack wrote 11,000 words so you know he's adding about 2 or 3,000 extra words here and

then in his third week he wrote a whopping 21,000 words that week and I don't actually have the screenshots here but in his first 5 weeks inside the guild he wrote around 70,000 words of his novel and this is particularly impressive considering he just started writing his first story late last year he's never written anything before so I actually pulled him onto a Zoom call and asked him how he's been a to use the guild to make so much progress as a new writer and here's what he said

accountability is just a huge part of it I I recognized that I was just riding in a vacuum before looking at this like I said I wanted to be wise about the whole thing I need to Define myself a community being part of the community has just been huge it's been very supportive I I love the interactions I I'm kind of feel like a sponge just absorbing what I'm getting there it's a delight when you talk about the joy of writing it's just enhanced it for me inside the guild we've built an

accountability method that helps our writers be more productive and to not just write faster but to also be writing better stories and to be writing with more joy as well we've even built a peer feedback system inside the guild as well which lets you get really useful critiques and edits on your writing from your fellow Guild members to help you improve your story and all this has led to amazing results like Arwin who finished his first draft of 107,000 words or Cole who recently crossed over

the 50,000 word Mark of his story or Beth who met her daily writing goal 52 days in a row while being inside the or Jonathan who has found his incentive to write has gone way up because he now has so many more new sources of inspiration and feedback from the other members it's really showing me that finding the right Community is like activating a cheat code for your writing so if you would like to join us in the guild Community then go to jar.com Guild to apply okay so as you're going through

the process of writing your first draft there's probably going to be a time where you feel stuck you just feel like you can't make progress with your story and you start to feel a bit frustrated about the book you're working on here are the ways that I get out of this situation when I faced it before because there have been so many times over you know the 12 years I've been writing where I've just not felt like the words were flowing the first thing I do is I re outline I look at where the story you

know is heading at this stage and I ask myself is that the right direction for things or do I need to shift them into another Direction entirely I also try to Workshop my ideas with other fellow writers again that's something that's really cool about the guild is you've got this community of other writers around you so that you can you know quickly get feedback on different ideas and different directions for your story and sometimes it's just through the act of actually explaining your problem that

you're facing to other writers that leads to these really good breakthroughs and unlocks so one of the things we do in the guild is we actually have a private weekly podcast where people uh basically send in their writing questions to me and I answer them every week and I've actually found multiple instances now of writers who have sent in their question to me I answer on the podcast and they go you know that was a really useful answer but I kind of feel like just asking you the question help

me fix it and it's no longer an issue for me right now the other thing you can be applying and this is more on a scene by scene level so if you find yourself you know going to write a scene and that's just not that interesting or it's just not that motivating to you ask yourself this question which I got from Peter Armstrong who is one of Brandon serson editors why will this scene be somebody's favorite scene so for example you know in Kingdom of Dragons there was a sequence where Zora and her baby

dragon dapple along with Zora's boyfriend at this time in the story mother are basically going on a boat between this sort of tropical island nation back to their homeland of Alaya and this was sort of a necessary transition scene to have in the story but when I was initially thinking about it I wasn't you know so enthused about it I was like ah it's just kind of a travel sequence it just needs to be there but I'm not really feeling too jazzed about it and I stopped myself right and I said no no no no no hang on

a second there's got to be something about this scene that makes it really really fun both for you to write and for other people to read so I sort of sat down and I was thinking about it and I went this is actually a really rare moment where Zora is just able have like a bit of happiness and carefreeness with with dapple and with her sort of love interest mson at the time in the story and you know it's an opportunity for us to just be playing and to be like what a cool adventure where on you know we've

got a baby dragon here we're sailing across the seas maybe Zora and mson are doing a bit of training on the ship because they're Rangers apprentices and their gr master Withers is there you know taking them through their paces and then I decided to kind of come up with this idea of well what happens if like a storm you know capsizes the boat and and zor and mson are throwing off and they have to you know try to get back to the boat and everything and all of a sudden this scene that initially just felt like

a bit of filler it turned into one of my favorite scenes from the story and I think that when you read through it and you know it's something that happens in the first few chapters of the book you sense that Joy come across and it actually feels like a really you know enjoyable scene to experience so you can see like how much I'm lighting up as I talk about this idea right and so you can really see like that's the power of this question right it takes a scene that maybe feels a little bit boring and

you tell yourself no no no no no there's going to be something I do in this scene that makes it a really standout moment for one reader it might not be for every reader but how can I Infuse something here that makes it more interesting for me to write the next question to be asking yourself if you've sort of gone through these first three steps and you still feel a little bit blocked is does this block actually reveal a problem so I like to kind of believe believe that rers block doesn't exist I think that if

you give rers block too much power and you think that this concept really exists out there it doesn't really have any positive benefit for you it just kind of means that sometimes you feel like you've been struck down by this Supernatural entity which I don't think is useful at all I like to think about writing as a very nuts and bolts you know crafting thing a plumber doesn't get plumbers block where they're like you know what I'm not feeling that motivated to declog this toilet today

they just rock up and they fix the problem and they continue on with their life I think more useful than thinking about writers block is actually split it out into these three different categories of plot block energy block or life block so plot block is like you're not feeling excited to move forward with the story and it's not that you facing rers block this sort of nebulous you know spiritual concept it's probably that your subconscious recognizes that there's something wrong

in the story and that if you keep moving forward you're just going to create more problems for you to solve later on and you're going to end up having to rewrite it anyway so whenever you face these moments in your story and heck I literally had one of these moments the other day right where I'm currently through the process of writing the sequel to Kingdom of Dragons I'm about 90% of the way through the first draft of Sor of the sun which is book two in this series and I just found

myself feeling really resistant to a couple of scenes I was writing leading up to the final climactic battle and I realized oh it's because you're forcing this character to do something that makes no sense for her it doesn't have any consistency with her personality traits that you've established before before it doesn't really work with her Arc that's why you feel resistant so I've actually changed that scene around a little bit recently and yesterday when I was writing it through I just had a

big grit on my face cuz I was like oh yes this dialogue it's flowing so much better this character is being you know her usual kind of witty Snappy like kind of lowkey evil self and it was just so much more fun to write the next type of block is energy block so doesn't really matter how talented you are as a writer if you're running on like 2 hours of sleep and you're wondering why the writing isn't flowing it's probably because you got 2 hours of sleep so just assess that and ask yourself well am I

really facing a blockage in my story or do I just need to get some rest and to get some new nutrition in the other kind of block is a life block this one's a little bit harder but I have found for me personally and for other writers I've talked to you know sometimes stuff comes up in your life where you just need to process that or you need to engage with that first and it's difficult to actually do a creative project outside of it whether it is you know the the loss of a loved one or maybe you've lost

your job and and maybe you're going through some kind of heal thing at the moment sometimes you actually just need to focus on that first in order to get yourself into the right mental head space to write and then the last thing of all of these approaches fail is to be reconnecting with your sources of inspiration so you know whatever book it is you're working on right now there's probably some things that inspired you to write that book go back to those Source materials whether it is you know

a book you read as a kid or whether it's a movie that was really inspirational to you and just try to reconnect with your joy for what made that story great often that's going to give you the clues you need to burst through the particular blockage that you're facing right now and continue on with writing the first draft and then the last thing I would say about this phenomenon of feeling stuck is that you know if you're 20,000 words into your novel 30,000 words into it or whatever it is and you're thinking

about giving up I really strongly encourage you to just push through to completion for your first novel you are going to learn so much more from finishing a crappy 80,000 word novel and then learning from that maybe moving on to another project rather than like starting and stopping five half complete novels in the same time and again this is why I really do strongly suggest that you pick a relatively easy and small scope for your first story to make it easier to push through those moments of blockages the blocks you're going to

face on a 200,000 word fantasy epic that is the start of like a 10 book series they're probably going to be far more substantial and harder to overcome rather than the blocks you might face you know writing something like across thebroken Stars which is one point of view character a standalone story 80,000 words again think about how you can rig the game to make it easy rig the game to put the odds in your favor so for some more tips here to make the first draft writing process a bit easier I like to

start each day each day's writing session with a writer brief so this is basically where you know you'll just grab just grab a notebook a piece of paper whatever it is and you'll just jot down here are the bullet points for what I'm going to try to achieve with my writing today you know character does this then this happens maybe there's a line of dialogue in there just giving yourself a few minutes to sort of sort of warm up and to note down what you're going to write that day even if you

already have a very strong outline I find it so useful and it just saves me a lot of staring at the screen blankly trying to figure out what I'm going to write the next tip is to try to end each day's writing session before you exhaust your ideas so there's this great quote here from Ernest Hemingway where he says always stop while you are going good and don't think about it or worry about it until you start to write the next day that way your subconscious will work on it all the time but if you think about

it consciously or worry about it you will kill it and your brain will be tired before you start so what he's sort of talking about here is this practice of you know rather than writing until the end of your ideas try to stop a little bit before that so something I do on a very tactical level and it's a bit of a weird habit but I've found it very useful for other writers out there is I'll literally finish like halfway through a word or halfway through a sentence and I'll know where the next

couple of sentences are going so the next day I can come back I can sit down I can immediately finish that sentence and I feel like I've got this instant sense of momentum with my writing and then it's it's also worthwhile noting that your first draft doesn't have to do everything at once there was a great post from Timothy who's one of the members of my fantasy Writers Guild the other day where he was describing his approach where he would typically write and just focus on the dialogue in his

first draft he called it kind of scripting and he basically said you know I would just focus on getting the dialogue down and then I KN know that I would come back in my second or third draft and you know work the descriptions or the action around that and it's a way of sort of just quickly getting his ideas down onto the page I've actually done something very similar to this myself in the past so when I was writing the first draft of Kingdom of Dragons there's a whole subplot in this where

Roven who is the the spy who steals a dragon egg to infiltrate alium the floating city of the Dragon Riders to destroy it from within I knew that I wanted him to be communicating with his master um a guy called aridan who was sort of the emperor of this other nation and I was going to have this sort of system where they would communicate to each other by using this this token or this amulet that takes them into a shared vision of the past and I wanted these visions of the past to kind of convey the worldbuilding of my story and

to explain some important law that had happened 700 years prior to the events of the actual Kingdom of Dragons book itself but when I was writing the first draft in those scenes where it was Roven and arent inside the Visions I just focused on their dialogue that was it cuz I didn't exactly know how many of these scenes there were going to be even though I was outlining things you know sometimes I would realize oh it makes sense for Ren and aridan to have another conversation here or whatever and I just

wanted to focus on nailing that dialogue and getting that conversation and the tension right then in my second draft actually went back through I counted up all these scenes that were in the vision and I outlined a little almost mini Nolla that takes place in these Visions where it would sort of show this background story of these eldrich Gods who had controlled the world prior to the Dragon Riders and the sun wardens coming onto the scene um and it was a really fun thing to write because it allowed me to sort of Infuse this

element of World building and history into the background in in a way that made these conversation sces far more interesting and I I would posit that many people when they go back and reread this book they'll probably find a lot of new details and a lot of Easter eggs contained in those sequences and so what you can see there is I didn't focus on doing everything at once in my first draft I realized that look I'm going to have multiple drafts later down the line and we're going to talk about editing

later in this video as well but it just takes the pressure off a little bit it means that you're not feeling so constrained by having to achieve all these different things at once you you can just focus on getting the bones of your story down first and then come back in and add some more richness and more layers to it later on the other tip I would have here is to try to write at full intensity what do I mean by that well I like to think about your writing intensity as something that increases

when you're physically typing faster so more words are actually coming out onto the keyboard every minute when you're more focused you're generally more intense if you're like you know just staring at your computer screen and there's a blank wall behind rather than if you're in a busy Cafe and there's tons of stuff going on around you when you also have more story knowledge more knowledge of what's going to happen in your plot that helps you increase your intensity as well and when you're

focusing on the things that you enjoy about your writing that also helps with your intensity factor when you don't stop to Tinker and change and edit things in your story as you go along that also ramps up your intensity and using placeholders is another method as well to boost the intensity of your writing so rather than sitting there for 20 minutes trying to think up the perfect character name just use placeholders you can see here I use these curly brackets a lot to basically just put in a placeholder for a

character's name then later on when you're editing you can do control find in word or whatever other program you're using and you can come back through and change those later on I think in the first draft for Kingdom of Dragons I had something like 200 placeholders in there so you know it was probably a few hours of editing where I went back and actually fixed them but it meant that I could write in a far more liberated way in my first draft now the reason why intensity is so important here is

because I'm a firm believer that writing at 100% intensity so you know you're typing as fast as you can you're focused you've got lots of knowledge of your story you're not distracted typing at full intensity for 20 minutes writing at full intensity for 20 minutes I should say is so much better than 60 Minutes at 20% intensity So 20% intensity looks like you know you're doing a bit of writing here but then every few minutes you check your phone and then you Google something random on the internet maybe

you look at your emails you go off and make food or whatever that is a hard place to be productive there are some people where that's probably right and maybe that is useful for you but in my experience I write so much better when I just try to fully lock in and even if that means I have a smaller amount of actual writing time I think it's a better approach because the thing is over time the duration that you'll be able to maintain your full intensity is going to be increasing here but you

really just want to be optimizing for attendance first and I know you might be thinking right now that like oh well if I write fast that reduces the quality in my experience that has not been the case I've found that the scenes I've ridden you know the ones where I've needed the least amount of editing and they've been the most fun to write and they have revealed the best things about my character and moved the story forward in the best way possible they have usually come when I'm writing really fast

because there's just something that kind of unlocks the inhibitions in your brain when you're writing fast and the story feels like it's flowing out of you sometimes when you go too slowly you're sort of picking your way over every single word and you're really thinking about things and it feels a little tight it doesn't feel organic or natural as it does when you write with full intensity and with full speed and then the last thing say when it comes to writing your first draft here and you know obviously

there's a lot of other technical stuff as well that I cover in my videos too like how to write good dialogue or pros and all that sort of thing as well but in terms of the sort of habits and the mindset when it comes to writing your first draft here you just need to keep going that's all it really comes down to here if you think about writing an 80,000 word story so you know that is pretty much exactly the length of across the broken Stars I think it's what is it work out to be in total it works out to

be about 350 pages in this book an 880,000 words story if you just write 500 words per day you are going to have your first draft done in under half a year if you write 1,000 words per day you're going to have your first draft done in under 3 months so to quote from confucious here it does not matter how slowly you go as long as you do not stop all righty it is now time to move on to the fourth and final stage when it comes to writing your fantasy novel and that is the editing stage this is absolutely

critical to get right writing in my mind is more about rewriting and it's in revision that you really get to shape the story and turn it into its best possible possible form so when it comes to editing the key principle you want to follow here is to work from the biggest changes first to the smallest changes you want to avoid coming in and you know tweaking around with the sentences or polishing up some dialogue and doing that for a couple of weeks and then realizing oh wait I actually need to cut

out these five chapters from the story which I've just spent all this time polishing the pros on you don't want to be rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic you want to be coming in and identifying what are the biggest problems to solve in this story first then solve those and then drill down into the smaller issues as you get deeper into the editing process so my editing process is something that has evolved a lot over the years and continues to evolve and be different from story to story but generally I like

to begin by letting the story rest after I finish the first draft I'll give it a few weeks and in that time I'll ideally try to go work on something else maybe it's a short Nolla I've actually written a bunch of short novellas and short stories between my major books so if you go to my website you can actually read those all for free you know I've got a little prequel to fires of the Dead for example I've got a little prequel to The Thunder Heist and I've even got a little

prequel to Across the broken Stars I don't have anything at this stage that is a sort of Side Story to Kingdom of Dragons but I might write that at some point in the future and I find that it's really nice to just do a little pallet cleanse and you know write something else whether it is a short story that's set earlier than my book or whether it's working on a completely different project instead then what I will do is I will read the entire story and take notes I like to actually try to print

off my story if I can or at the very least transfer it onto my Kindle and read it on there basically not read it on the screen that I wrote it on because it just helps to create a layer of objectivity and helps me look at the story with much clearer eyes once I've gone through and I've taken all those notes I will then work on my second draft the focus here is on Big Picture structural fixes you know things like the character arcs or whether these chapters need to be rearranged or if new

scenes need to be written or that sort of thing the third draft is all about specific editing passes I'll get into that in a little bit more detail later on and usually at this stage this is when I will then open it up and give it to early readers to look at I'll talk about my early reader process in a bit once I've got their feedback I'm going to be incorporating that into my fourth draft and then I will usually be trying to hire a pro editor at this point in order to do the last big tweaks in the

story before my fifth draft which is looking at the final polishes the you know word smithing trying to make the pros as nice as possible and all of that fun sort of polishing up stuff so I'm going to go through these steps in a little bit more detail here so in terms of actually making the notes on my first draft here what you really want to be thinking about is how can I trick my brain into thinking that I did not write this thing so what I like to do to achieve this is to actually either print

my story out or if it's really massive like you know Kingdom of Dragons is prohibitively expensive if I'm printing out like 700 Pages or whatever transfer it to your Kindle so your Kindle actually has this cool feature where you can literally email a Word document over to your Kindle and you can then read it through there as an ebook and ideally is as well you might want to think about using a different font again to make it more foreign to give you a more objective view of the story and pretend

you are an external editor in this stage pretend you didn't write it I will literally treat this like I am externally editing someone else's story you know I'll be like you didn't do this thing here or seems like the story is struggling with this like I'll literally write it the same way I would as if I was editing someone else's work I'd like to divide my first draft notes into sort of General notes on the story then structure specific notes character notes World building related notes plot notes

and then I'll have specific comments for each individual chapter as well tell you what let me actually show you what my editing notes look like for Kingdom of Dragons let me get that up now so you can see here here are my editing notes for Kingdom of Dragons I've got about 20,000 words worth of notes Here for myself which is pretty excessive but I like to go detailed with my editing and you'll see here I've kind of laid out the particular editing process I want to go through for this book you can see

here I'm mentioning the added scenes that I might need to add into the story and then if you scroll down you can see I've got some general notes Here character related notes World notes plot notes I've got this Arc phrases section as well which is basically you know an arc phrase is kind of a a motto that gets repeated several times throughout the story ideally as a way to sort of develop emotion and reflect a character's growth as well you can see me playing around with a couple of

different Arc phrases as well some of them which made its way into the story too and then you can see I've got specific notes on each chapter for some chapters it's only a few little things here like okay you need to mention that tril is a tropical island as early as possible that's literally you know a note that happens pretty early on in the story some notes are much bigger to figure out like there's where is it there's some note I made up here about like do I need to cut out you know the
